---
title: "Lesson 3:  Convergence for Sequences of Random Variables"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_the-weak-law-of-large-numbers.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_the-weak-law-of-large-numbers.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-the-weak-law-of-large-numbers_module3_lesson3.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## Slides on Limits of Deterministic Sequences {#sec-limits-deterministic-sequences}

For an ordinary sequence of numbers, the usual notion of a limit applies. For example,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{n} &= 0 \\
\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac12\right)^n &= 0 \\
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{2n^2+3}{3n^3-7} &= 0 \\
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{2n^2+3}{3n^2-7} &= \frac23
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, if

$$
a_n=\frac{n-1}{n}=1-\frac1n
$$

then

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n=1
$$

## Limits of Random Variables {#sec-limits-random-variables}

Consider a sequence of RVs
$X_1,X_2,X_3,\ldots$
[The expression $\lim_{n\to\infty}X_n$ is not meaningful until a particular notion of convergence for random variables has been specified]{.mark}.

::: {#def-convergence-in-probability}
## Convergence in Probability

The sequence of random variables $X_1,X_2,X_3,\ldots$ converges in probability to a random variable $X$ if, for every $\varepsilon>0$,

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert X_n-X\rvert>\varepsilon\right)
=0
$$

We write

$$
X_n\xrightarrow{P}X
$$
:::

::: {#exm-convergence-to-a-constant}
## Convergence to a Constant

The limiting random variable may be constant. For example, $X=3$ with probability one can be written as

$$
\mathbb{P}(X=x)
=
\begin{cases}
1, & x=3 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Thus, convergence in probability to the constant $3$ is written as

$$
X_n\xrightarrow{P}3
$$
:::

## Integrating over an Event {#sec-integrating-over-event}

Let

$$
A=\{x:0\leq x\leq 2\}
$$

Then the same integral can be written as

$$
\int_0^2 f(x)\,dx
=
\int_A f(x)\,dx
$$

More generally, if $X$ has probability density function $f_X$, then

$$
\mathbb{P}(X\in A)
=
\int_A f_X(x)\,dx
$$

::: {#exm-sine-of-an-exponential-random-variable}
## Probability Involving a Transformation

Suppose that

$$
X\sim\operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)
$$

We want to find

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert\sin(X)\rvert>\frac12\right)
$$

### Method 1: Find the Distribution of the Transformation {#sec-transformation-method}

Define

$$
Y=\lvert\sin(X)\rvert
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert\sin(X)\rvert>\frac12\right)
&=\mathbb{P}\!\left(Y>\frac12\right) \\
&=\int_{1/2}^{\infty}f_Y(y)\,dy
\end{aligned}
$$

### Method 2: Integrate over the Relevant Region {#sec-direct-region-method}

The region in which $\lvert\sin(x)\rvert>1/2$ consists of repeated intervals. Since $X$ is non-negative,

$$
\begin{aligned}
A
&=\left\{x\geq 0:\lvert\sin(x)\rvert>\frac12\right\} \\
&=\bigcup_{m=0}^{\infty}
\left(
 m\pi+\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac12\right),
 m\pi+\pi-\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac12\right)
\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert\sin(X)\rvert>\frac12\right)
&=
\int_{\sin^{-1}(1/2)}^{\pi-\sin^{-1}(1/2)}
 f_X(x)\,dx \\
&\quad+
\int_{\pi+\sin^{-1}(1/2)}^{2\pi-\sin^{-1}(1/2)}
 f_X(x)\,dx
+\cdots \\
&=
\int_{\{x:\lvert\sin(x)\rvert>1/2\}}
 f_X(x)\,dx
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

## A General Probability Inequality {#sec-general-probability-inequality}

::: {#thm-generalized-markov-inequality}
## Generalized Markov Inequality

Let $X$ be a random variable, let $g$ be a non-negative function, and let $c>0$. Then

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(g(X)\geq c\right)
\leq
\frac{\mathbb{E}[g(X)]}{c}
$$

When $g(x)=\lvert x\rvert$, this gives Markov's inequality

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert X\rvert\geq c\right)
\leq
\frac{\mathbb{E}[\lvert X\rvert]}{c}
$$
:::

::: {#prf-generalized-markov-inequality}
## Proof

Let

$$
A=\{x:g(x)\geq c\}
$$

Because $g$ is non-negative,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[g(X)]
&=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}g(x)f_X(x)\,dx \\
&=\int_A g(x)f_X(x)\,dx
+
\underbrace{\int_{A^c}g(x)f_X(x)\,dx}_{\displaystyle \geq 0} \\
&\geq\int_A g(x)f_X(x)\,dx \\
&\geq\int_A c f_X(x)\,dx
&& \text{because }g(x)\geq c\text{ on }A \\
&=c\int_A f_X(x)\,dx \\
&=c\,\mathbb{P}\!\left(g(X)\geq c\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by $c>0$ gives

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(g(X)\geq c\right)
\leq
\frac{\mathbb{E}[g(X)]}{c}
$$
:::

## Chebyshev's Inequality {#sec-chebyshev-inequality}

::: {#thm-chebyshev-inequality}
## Chebyshev's Inequality

Let $X$ be a random variable with

$$
\mathbb{E}[X]=\mu
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sigma^2<\infty
$$

For any $k>0$,

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert X-\mu\rvert\geq k\sigma\right)
\leq
\frac{1}{k^2}
$$

Equivalently,

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert X-\mu\rvert<k\sigma\right)
\geq
1-\frac{1}{k^2}
$$

The second form bounds the probability that $X$ lies within $k$ standard deviations of its mean.
:::

::: {#prf-chebyshev-inequality}
## Proof

Apply the generalized Markov inequality with

$$
g(X)=\color{red}{(X-\mu)^2}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
c=\color{red}{k^2\sigma^2}
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}\!\left(\lvert X-\mu\rvert\geq k\sigma\right)
&=
\mathbb{P}\!\left(
\underbrace{\color{red}{(X-\mu)^2}}_{\color{red}{g(X)}}
\geq
\underbrace{\color{red}{k^2\sigma^2}}_{\color{red}{c}}
\right) \\
&\leq
\frac{\mathbb{E}[(X-\mu)^2]}{k^2\sigma^2} \\
&=
\frac{\sigma^2}{k^2\sigma^2} \\
&=
\frac{1}{k^2}
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

## The Weak Law of Large Numbers {#sec-weak-law-large-numbers}

::: {#thm-weak-law-large-numbers}
## Weak Law of Large Numbers

Suppose that $X_1,X_2,X_3,\ldots$ are independent and identically distributed random variables from any distribution satisfying

$$
\mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(X_i)=\sigma^2<\infty
$$

Define the sample mean by

$$
\overline{X}_n
=
\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i
$$

Then

$$
\overline{X}_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu
$$
:::

The mean and variance of the sample mean are

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}_n]
&=\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right] \\
&=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\mathbb{E}[X_i] \\
&=\mu
\end{aligned}
$$

and, using independence,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}_n)
&=\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right) \\
&=\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n\operatorname{Var}(X_i) \\
&=\frac{\sigma^2}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus the standard deviation of the sample mean is

$$
\sigma_{\overline{X}_n}
=
\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

::: {#prf-weak-law-large-numbers}
## Proof

Let $\varepsilon>0$. Chebyshev's inequality gives

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(
\lvert\overline{X}_n-\mu\rvert
\geq
k\sigma_{\overline{X}_n}
\right)
\leq
\frac{1}{k^2}
$$

Since $\sigma_{\overline{X}_n}=\sigma/\sqrt{n}$,

$$
\mathbb{P}\!\left(
\lvert\overline{X}_n-\mu\rvert
\geq
\frac{k\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\right)
\leq
\frac{1}{k^2}
$$

Choose

$$
k=\frac{\varepsilon\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}
$$

so that $k\sigma/\sqrt{n}=\varepsilon$. Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{P}\!\left(
\lvert\overline{X}_n-\mu\rvert\geq\varepsilon
\right)
&\leq
\frac{1}{\left(\varepsilon\sqrt{n}/\sigma\right)^2} \\
&=
\frac{\sigma^2}{\varepsilon^2n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking limits,

$$
\begin{aligned}
0
&\leq
\lim_{n\to\infty}
\mathbb{P}\!\left(
\lvert\overline{X}_n-\mu\rvert\geq\varepsilon
\right) \\
&\leq
\lim_{n\to\infty}
\frac{\sigma^2}{\varepsilon^2n} \\
&=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence,

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}
\mathbb{P}\!\left(
\lvert\overline{X}_n-\mu\rvert\geq\varepsilon
\right)
=0
$$

which means

$$
\overline{X}_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu
$$
:::

## Examples {#sec-weak-law-examples}

::: {#exm-weak-law-exponential}
## Exponential Random Variables

If

$$
X_1,X_2,X_3,\ldots
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)
$$

then $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=1/\lambda$, so the Weak Law of Large Numbers gives

$$
\overline{X}_n\xrightarrow{P}\frac1\lambda
$$
:::

::: {#exm-weak-law-gamma}
## Gamma Random Variables

If

$$
X_1,X_2,X_3,\ldots
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the shape parameter and $\beta$ is the rate parameter, then

$$
\mathbb{E}[X_i]=\frac{\alpha}{\beta}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\overline{X}_n\xrightarrow{P}\frac{\alpha}{\beta}
$$
:::


## Reflection
